title: Characterizing Cochlear implant artefact removal from EEG recordings using a real human model
author: Undurraga, Van Yper, et al.

steps.title: Methods
steps.instruction: Electroencephography (EEG) recordings to electrically evoked sounds by cochlear implants CIs are challenging due to the presence of large electrical artefacts caused by electrical stimulation. This is particularly difficult when stimuli are presented at clinical stimulation rates—≈ 900 pulses per second (pps)—in which both temporal and spatial (different EEG electrodes) recordings are strongly contaminated.

steps.title: Methods
steps.instruction: The next set of experiments aimed to investigate the extent to which electrical artefacts could be removed from realistic recordings under electrical stimulation. To this end, a cadaveric human head was implanted with a CI and EEG recordings were made under several conditions, described in the next sections. These recordings allowed us to characterize several types of realistic electrical artefacts in a controlled environment. Furthermore, a large number of simulated neural responses were imposed to these recordings allowing us to investigate the advantages and limitations of the artefact removal method used in this study. The method combines interpolation—blanking of electrical artefacts via linear interpolation between time samples preceding the electrical pulses [1]— and denoising source separation (DSS) —a spatial filter that partitions components into highly and weakly reproducible ones [2].

steps.title: Methods
steps.instruction: The large number of conditions tested in this study would have been impossible to carry out in human CI listeners. However, examples from CI listeners are also provided in this study and fully reported in the associated research article [3].

steps.title: Methods
steps.instruction: Cadaveric recordings were conducted at the University of Cambridge. This was approved by the ethical board of Cambridge University (19/NE/0366) and complied with the Human Tissue Act (2004).
